summary: Gruber details a confusing UI problem in macOS's Software Update settings when users want to stay on macOS 15 Sequoia rather than upgrading to macOS 26 Tahoe. The 'ⓘ' info buttons next to both the Sequoia update and Tahoe upgrade open the same sheet, which defaults to having Tahoe selected, making it appear that clicking 'Update Now' for Sequoia would actually install Tahoe. While the main 'Update Now' button does correctly install the Sequoia update, the shared info panel creates unnecessary confusion and risk of accidental upgrades. Gruber himself is deliberately staying on Sequoia because he finds Tahoe's UI aesthetically poor, with its rounded window corners and redesigned app icons.
key_points: The 'ⓘ' buttons in different sections of Software Update all open the same sheet with the same default selections, rather than showing info specific to their section
The checked items in the info sheet have no bearing on what the main 'Update Now' and 'Upgrade Now' buttons install, but do control what gets installed from within that sheet itself
Gruber is deliberately staying on macOS 15 Sequoia not for compatibility reasons but because he thinks Tahoe's UI is 'goofy looking' with Fisher-Price-style corner radiuses
The actual 'Update Now' button does correctly install the Sequoia update despite the confusing info panel, as confirmed by a brave Mastodon user
money_quotes: My main MacBook Pro is still running MacOS 15 Sequoia — not because of any compatibility issues, but simply because I think the Tahoe user interface is goofy looking.
I'm luxuriating with a MacOS UI with well-crafted app icons and windows that don't have Fisher-Price-style corner radiuses.
I don't know what the i in the 'ⓘ' button is supposed to stand for, but it isn't intuitive.
It sure makes it seem like we need to be more careful than we actually do if we want to stick with MacOS 15 Sequoia for now.

So if you click the 'ⓘ' in the 'Other Updates' section, because you want to update to the latest version of MacOS 15 Sequoia and see what other updates are being installed, and you don't pay attention to the checked version of MacOS in that sheet, you can inadvertently upgrade to Tahoe.
key_insight: Apple's Software Update UI creates unnecessary confusion and risk by having all info buttons open the same shared sheet with Tahoe pre-selected, regardless of which update section the user clicked from.
